prompt: Why does every feature set need a Return or Throw?
actual: Every feature set needs a `Return` or `Throw` because it ensures that the application has a defined outcome. The `Return` action specifies the result of the feature set, such as a status code and data, while the `Throw` action signals an error condition. This makes the application predictable and ensures that all execution paths have a clear endpoint.
expected: Every feature set produces a status object that the runtime hands back to whatever invoked it (HTTP response, event ack, action result). `Return` produces a success path; `Throw` produces a failure path with a status code. The compiler rejects feature sets that fall off the end — there's no implicit "nothing" status, because every caller needs to know what happened.
